en.extract:
The blackish oystercatcher (Haematopus ater) is a species of wading bird in the oystercatcher family Haematopodidae. It is found in Argentina, Chile, the Falkland Islands and Peru, and is a vagrant to Uruguay. The population is estimated at 15,000–80,000.


Description
The blackish oystercatcher's plumage is slaty-black with wings and back rather dark brown. The long bill is blood-red and the legs are white. The sexes are similar in appearance. The bird is easily overlooked on a rocky shore, its dark colour blending with the rocks on which it walks as it forages, and it does not draw attention to itself. Its presence, however, can easily be detected by its loud and distinctive warning calls. Its song, when given in duet, consists of an excited chatter of piping whistles. Calls include notes that sound like "pip"  and "peeeeyeeee".


Distribution and habitat
The blackish oystercatcher is native to the coasts of Argentina, Chile, the Falkland Islands and Peru, and it is a vagrant to Uruguay. Its natural habitats are rocky shores. It feeds in the intertidal zone on rocky shorelines, in rockpools and on pebble beaches. Rarely, it can be found on sandy beaches hunting for mole crabs.


Status
The IUCN rates the blackish oystercatcher as being of "Least Concern". It is not clear whether the population is increasing or decreasing, but the bird has a very large range and a total population estimated to be somewhere between 15,000 and 80,000 individuals.


Gallery


See also
Black oystercatcher


References
 Media related to Haematopus ater at Wikimedia Commons

Mundo Azul 2011. Mundo Azul species profile sheet: Blackish oystercatcher

The '''blackish oystercatcher''' ('''''Haematopus ater''''') is a species of [[wader|wading bird]] in the oystercatcher [[Family (biology)|family]] [[Haematopodidae]]. It is found in [[Argentina]], [[Chile]], the [[Falkland Islands]] and [[Peru]], and is a [[vagrancy in birds|vagrant]] to [[Uruguay]]. The population is estimated at 15,000–80,000.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.birdlife.org/datazone/species/index.html?action=SpcHTMDetails.asp&sid=3098&m=0|title = New Species Protection Code of Conduct guides EU businesses towards nature protection|date = 28 October 2021}}</ref>

==Description==
The blackish oystercatcher's plumage is slaty-black with wings and back rather dark brown. The long bill is blood-red and the legs are white. The sexes are similar in appearance. The bird is easily overlooked on a rocky shore, its dark colour blending with the rocks on which it walks as it forages, and it does not draw attention to itself. Its presence, however, can easily be detected by its loud and distinctive warning calls. Its song, when given in duet, consists of an excited chatter of piping whistles. Calls include notes that sound like "pip"  and "peeeeyeeee".

==Distribution and habitat==
The blackish oystercatcher is native to the coasts of Argentina, Chile, the Falkland Islands and Peru, and it is a vagrant to Uruguay.<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021" /> Its natural habitats are rocky shores. It feeds in the [[intertidal zone]] on rocky shorelines, in rockpools and on pebble beaches. Rarely, it can be found on sandy beaches hunting for [[mole crab]]s.<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://mundoazul.org/habitats-species/shore-birds-waterfowl/blackish-oystercatcher-haematopus-ater/ |title=Blackish Oystercatcher – Haematopus ater | mundoazul |access-date=2011-02-03 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110224065042/http://mundoazul.org/habitats-species/shore-birds-waterfowl/blackish-oystercatcher-haematopus-ater/ |archive-date=2011-02-24 }}</ref>

==Status==
The [[IUCN]] rates the blackish oystercatcher as being of "[[Least Concern]]". It is not clear whether the population is increasing or decreasing, but the bird has a very large range and a total population estimated to be somewhere between 15,000 and 80,000 individuals.<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021" />
